title: Folding
subtitle: Hide regions of text — manual, syntax, indent, marker.
summary: Folding hides ranges behind a one-line summary. Folds can be created manually ({key:z}{key:f}), or generated by syntax/indent/marker rules. {key:z}{key:o}/{key:z}{key:c} open/close, {key:z}{key:R}/{key:z}{key:M} open/close all.

blocks.text: Folding lets you collapse a range of lines behind a one-line summary — useful for navigating large files or hiding boilerplate.

rows: Open / close one level (across all folds)
You create folds by hand with {key:z}{key:f}
Folds delimited by marker strings (default `{{{` and `}}}`)
Auto-set in diff mode — folds unchanged sections

blocks.title: Why Folds Annoy Some People
blocks.text:
Folds aren't *content* — they're a view layer. They can:

  • Suddenly close when you move into a folded region (depending on `foldopen`).
  • Cause unexpected jumps when motions land on fold boundaries.
  • Surprise newcomers who don't know what {key:z}{key:M} or auto-folding did.

If you don't want folds, `:set nofoldenable` (or {key:z}{key:i}) toggles them off entirely without losing the definitions. That's the common "calm down, folds" setting in many vimrcs.

blocks.title: Folds are presentation metadata over immutable lines
blocks.text: A fold doesn't modify the buffer. It's a `(start_line, end_line, state)` triple stored in window-side metadata, and the renderer hides those rows when state is closed. Fold methods (manual, indent, marker, syntax, expr) are different constructors for that triple — manual is user-supplied; indent computes from leading whitespace; marker scans for `{{{` / `}}}`; syntax asks the highlighter; expr evaluates a Vim-script expression per line.
